g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Swapping in Operating Systems. We'll explore how OS manages memory efficiently.
Physical RAM is limited, but we need to run many processes. This creates a memory shortage problem in computing.
Swapping temporarily moves processes between RAM and disk. This allows running more processes than physical memory can hold.
g.text: Swapping temporarily moves entire processes between RAM and secondary storage (disk).
Increases degree of multiprogramming | Allows larger processes to run

g.data-narration: Swap Out: OS copies the entire process address space from RAM to disk. The memory is then freed for other processes.
g.text: Copies: Code, Data, Stack, and PCB from RAM to disk

g.data-narration: Swap In: OS copies the process from disk back into available RAM. The process can then resume execution.
g.text: Process resumes execution when its turn comes or waiting event occurs

g.data-narration: The Medium-Term Scheduler (swapper) decides which processes to swap. It balances between active and idle processes.
g.text: Goal: Maximize ready processes in memory, increase CPU utilization

g.data-narration: The main cost of swapping is TIME. Disk transfer is millions of times slower than RAM access. This creates significant overhead.
g.text: Disk is MUCH slower than RAM:
Total Swap Time = Transfer Time + Seek Time + Rotational Latency

g.data-narration: Let's calculate: 100MB process at 50MB/s transfer rate takes 2 seconds just to swap out. That's 4 seconds total!
g.text: Process Size: 100 MB | Disk Transfer Rate: 50 MB/s
Total Context Switch Time with Swap: 4 seconds!
Strategy: Only swap processes that will be idle long enough to justify this overhead

g.data-narration: There are two variants: Standard swapping moves entire processes, while modern systems use page-level swapping for efficiency.
With paging, only inactive memory pages are swapped out. This reduces transfer time and allows partially loaded processes to run.
Real example: 4GB RAM running 10 processes of 1GB each. Process D is idle, so swap it out to make room for Process K.
g.text: Now 1GB RAM free! Can load new process or give memory to existing ones

g.data-narration: Swapping enables systems to run more processes than physical memory allows. It prevents crashes and improves throughput.
Key summary: Swapping moves processes between RAM and disk. It's managed by the medium-term scheduler with significant time overhead.
In conclusion, swapping is fundamental to memory management. It allows OS to overcome physical RAM limitations, though modern systems use more efficient paging.
g.text: Swapping is a fundamental memory management technique that enables operating systems to overcome the
physical limitations of RAM, facilitating higher multiprogramming and better system throughput.
While pure swapping is rare today, its concepts form the foundation for virtual memory systems.
